title: DRCT Diffusion Reconstruction Contrastive Training towards Universal Detection of Diffusion Generated Images
abstract: Diffusion models have made significant strides in visual content generation but also raised increasing demands on generated image detection. Existing detection methods have achieved considerable progress, but they usually suffer a significant decline in accuracy when detecting images generated by an unseen diffusion model. In this paper, we seek to address the generalizability of generated image detectors from the perspective of hard sample classification. The basic idea is that if a classifier can distinguish generated images that closely resemble real ones, then it can also effectively detect less similar samples, potentially even those produced by a different diffusion model. Based on this idea, we propose Diffusion Reconstruction Contrastive Learning (DRCT), a universal framework to enhance the generalizability of the existing detectors. DRCT generates hard samples by high-quality diffusion reconstruction and adopts contrastive training to guide the learning of diffusion artifacts. In addition, we have built a million-scale dataset, DRCT- 2M, including 16 types diffusion models for the evaluation of generalizability of detection methods. Extensive experimental results show that detectors enhanced with DRCT achieve over a 10% accuracy improvement in cross-set tests. The code, models, and dataset will soon be available at https://github.com/beibuwandeluori/DRCT.
impact: Our work aims to advance the field of Machine Learning by enhancing the detection of generated images through the proposed DRCT framework. This advancement has significant social implications. On the positive side, our method improves the ability to detect generated images, which is crucial for curbing misinformation, regulating the usage of generated content, and enhancing the credibility of digital media. However, there is a potential risk that attackers could exploit DRCT to create harder-to-detect fake images in an adversarial setting. To address this, we propose strategies such as access control and continuous model updates. Recognizing the dual-edged nature of this technology, we urge the research community to strive for minimizing negative impacts while capitalizing on its positive contributions.

query_input.scenario: Automated content moderation systems screening uploads for AI-generated content
query_input.issue: False negatives enable generated images to bypass detection and disseminate false information.
hazard: Reconstruction-error-based detection misclassifies genuine images with textures that mimic diffusion model residuals.
exposure: Content moderators using AI tools to identify and remove policy-violating AI-generated images on social platforms.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Moderators rely solely on detector alerts; high false-alarm rates breed alarm fatigue, causing dismissal of true generated-image flags.
hipporag_result.impact: Undetected AI-generated images circulate, spreading disinformation and undermining information integrity across the platform.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Reconstruction-error-based detection misclassifies genuine images with diffusion-like textures, through content moderators using AI tools, triggering alarm fatigue and dismissal of true flags, leading to undetected AI-generated images spreading disinformation and undermining integrity.

dose_response: Reconstruction-error-based detection fails to catch AI-generated images, and automated moderation without human verification lets them spread, enabling wide disinformation and undermining platform integrity.
vulnerability: Automated moderation lacks human verification, allowing AI-generated content with misleading textures to pass through.
impact: False negatives enable wide dissemination of disinformation, manipulating public discourse and undermining platform integrity.
